(**NY 5**) is a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") that extends for 370.80 miles (596.74 km) across the state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It begins at the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in the [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York") town of [Ripley](/wiki/Ripley,_New_York "Ripley, New York") and passes through [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York"), [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York"), and several other smaller cities and communities on its way to downtown [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") in [Albany County](/wiki/Albany_County,_New_York "Albany County, New York"), where it terminates at [U.S. Route 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York") (US 9), here routed along the service roads for [Interstate 787](/wiki/Interstate_787 "Interstate 787") (I-787). Prior to the construction of the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway"), it was one of two main east–west highways traversing upstate [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), the other being [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York"). West of New York, the road continues as [Pennsylvania Route 5](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_5 "Pennsylvania Route 5") (PA 5) to [Erie](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania").

**New York State Route 5** (**NY 5**) is a [state highway](/wiki/State_highway "State highway") that extends for 370.80 miles (596.74 km) across the state of [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") in the United States. It begins at the [Pennsylvania state line](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in the [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York") town of [Ripley](/wiki/Ripley,_New_York "Ripley, New York") and passes through [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York"), [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York"), and several other smaller cities and communities on its way to downtown [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") in [Albany County](/wiki/Albany_County,_New_York "Albany County, New York"), where it terminates at [U.S. Route 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York") (US 9), here routed along the service roads for [Interstate 787](/wiki/Interstate_787 "Interstate 787") (I-787). Prior to the construction of the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway"), it was one of two main east–west highways traversing upstate [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), the other being [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York"). West of New York, the road continues as [Pennsylvania Route 5](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_5 "Pennsylvania Route 5") (PA 5) to [Erie](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania").

NY 5 [overlaps](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)") with US 20 twice along its routing. The second, a 68-mile (109 km) overlap through western and central New York, is the second-longest concurrency in the state, stretching from [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York") in [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") east to the city of [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York") in [Cayuga County](/wiki/Cayuga_County,_New_York "Cayuga County, New York"). The concurrency is known locally as "Routes 5 and 20".[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3) As the route proceeds across the state, it also directly or indirectly meets every major north–south highway in [upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"), including all three north–south [Interstate Highways](/wiki/Interstate_Highway "Interstate Highway") ([I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") in [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(town),_New_York "Avon (town), New York"), [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") in Syracuse via [US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11_in_New_York "U.S. Route 11 in New York"), and [I-87](/wiki/Interstate_87_(New_York) "Interstate 87 (New York)") in Albany).

NY 5 was assigned in 1924 as a true cross-state highway, extending from the Pennsylvania state line in the west to the [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") state line in the east, mostly by way of modern US 20. At the time, modern NY 5 between Buffalo and Albany was designated as **New York State Route 5A**. By 1926, NY 5 was moved onto the routing of NY 5A while the old routing of NY 5 became [NY 7](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_7_(mid-1920s%E2%80%931927) "New York State Route 7 (mid-1920s–1927)"). It was truncated in 1927 to [Athol Springs](/wiki/Athol_Springs,_New_York "Athol Springs, New York") in the west and Albany in the east following the assignment of US 20, and again in 1930 to downtown Buffalo. NY 5 was reextended to the Pennsylvania state line c. 1932 by way of its old routing to Athol Springs, an old alignment of US 20, and a lakeside spur route of US 20 that had been assigned in 1930. Only local realignments have occurred since.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route description")]

Although it is no longer commonly used for long-distance travel, NY 5 is still regionally important. NY 5 is named [Main Street](/wiki/Main_Street_(Buffalo) "Main Street (Buffalo)") in [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), Erie Boulevard and West Genesee Street in [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), State Street in [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York"), and Central Avenue in [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), the state capital. It is a major local road in many other locations along its path. NY 5 runs concurrent to US 20 twice between its endpoints: for three miles (5  km) between [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York") and [Irving](/wiki/Irving,_New_York "Irving, New York") and for 68 miles (109 km) across western and central New York. At 67.6 miles (108 km) in length, the eastern [overlap](/wiki/Overlap_(road) "Overlap (road)") between US 20 and NY 5 is the second-longest surface-road concurrency in New York state, behind only the concurrency of [I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(east) "Interstate 86 (east)") and [NY 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") in the [Southern Tier](/wiki/Southern_Tier "Southern Tier").[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Entering_New_York_on_NY_5.jpg/250px-Entering_New_York_on_NY_5.jpg)](/wiki/File:Entering_New_York_on_NY_5.jpg)

The western terminus of NY 5 at the Pennsylvania state line, from where the first [reference](/wiki/Reference_marker_(New_York) "Reference marker (New York)") and [reassurance markers](/wiki/Reassurance_marker "Reassurance marker") on NY 5 eastbound are visible.

Maintenance of the majority of NY 5's 371 miles (597 km) is performed by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation"). However, locally owned and maintained sections exist in six cities. The city-maintained sections of NY 5 are in [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York") from [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16") north to the city line (except of the Goodell Street portion, which is state-maintained);[[4]](#cite_note-erieinv-4) in [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York") between the western city line and just west of [NY 635](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_635 "New York State Route 635");[[5]](#cite_note-onondagainv-5) in [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York") from Leland Avenue east to the city line;[[6]](#cite_note-oneidainv-6) in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam,_New_York "Amsterdam, New York") between Division and West Main streets;[[7]](#cite_note-amsterdam_quad-7) in [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York") from Washington Avenue to the eastern city line;[[8]](#cite_note-schenectadyinv-8) and the entirety of NY 5 within [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York").[[9]](#cite_note-albanyinv-9)

### Pennsylvania to Buffalo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pennsylvania to Buffalo")]

At the [New York–Pennsylvania border](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Pennsylvania_border "New York–Pennsylvania border") in Ripley, [PA 5](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Route_5 "Pennsylvania Route 5") becomes NY 5 upon entering New York. It very closely follows the shore of [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") through all of [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"). Once reaching the village of [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York") it briefly overlaps [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") until entering [Erie County](/wiki/Erie_County,_New_York "Erie County, New York") at the [Cattaraugus Reservation](/wiki/Cattaraugus_Reservation "Cattaraugus Reservation") and [NY 438](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_438 "New York State Route 438") where the roads once again split. Once in Erie County, it pulls slightly inward from the lake shore from [Brant](/wiki/Brant,_New_York "Brant, New York") to the hamlet of [Wanakah](/wiki/Wanakah,_New_York "Wanakah, New York"). Once past Wanakah, the road once again closely borders the lake shore and goes through steadily more heavily developed areas, particularly the [Ford](/wiki/Ford_Motor_Company "Ford Motor Company") Stamping Plant and the [Bethlehem Steel](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel "Bethlehem Steel") plant in the city of [Lackawanna](/wiki/Lackawanna,_New_York "Lackawanna, New York"). There the road becomes the Hamburg Turnpike and eight wind-powered turbines, which provide power to the national grid, are visible. Near the northern edge of the city, NY 5 begins to ascend onto an elevated roadway as it connects to Ridge Road and the [Buffalo and Erie County Botanical Gardens](/wiki/Buffalo_and_Erie_County_Botanical_Gardens "Buffalo and Erie County Botanical Gardens") by way of an interchange. Here, the route becomes the a [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") with exits for Ohio and Tifft streets and Fuhrmann Boulevard. After a quarter-mile, NY 5 passes seamlessly into the city of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/BuffaloSkyway.jpg/250px-BuffaloSkyway.jpg)](/wiki/File:BuffaloSkyway.jpg)

The Skyway (NY 5) as it passes over the [Buffalo River](/wiki/Buffalo_River_(New_York) "Buffalo River (New York)").

A short distance past the city line, NY 5 passes over the Union Ship Canal on a span of the elevated road known as the [Father Baker](/wiki/Father_Nelson_Baker "Father Nelson Baker") Bridge. North of the waterway, the elevated section of NY 5 gains a [frontage road](/wiki/Frontage_road "Frontage road") named Fuhrmann Boulevard. Both the service road and NY 5 run parallel to [Lake Erie](/wiki/Lake_Erie "Lake Erie") until the northern end of the Buffalo Outer Harbor. Here, the frontage roads end while NY 5 turns to the northeast, crossing the [Buffalo River](/wiki/Buffalo_River_(New_York) "Buffalo River (New York)") on the bridge called The Skyway, and entering downtown. On the north bank, the Skyway returns to a northerly routing as it passes [KeyBank Center](/wiki/KeyBank_Center "KeyBank Center"), located directly to the east, and [Buffalo and Erie County Naval & Military Park](/wiki/Buffalo_and_Erie_County_Naval_%26_Military_Park "Buffalo and Erie County Naval & Military Park"), situated to the west, and meets [I-190](/wiki/Interstate_190_(New_York) "Interstate 190 (New York)") at exit 7. Past the interchange, the Skyway ends and the route descends in elevation, becoming an [at-grade](/wiki/Intersection_(road) "Intersection (road)") roadway once more at Church Street in the shadow of [Buffalo City Hall](/wiki/Buffalo_City_Hall "Buffalo City Hall"). [NY 384](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_384 "New York State Route 384") begins here, following Delaware Avenue north into the heart of downtown, while NY 5 turns east onto Church.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Skyway_Skyline%2C_Buffalo%2C_New_York_-_20210908.jpg/250px-Skyway_Skyline%2C_Buffalo%2C_New_York_-_20210908.jpg)](/wiki/File:Skyway_Skyline,_Buffalo,_New_York_-_20210908.jpg)

Approaching downtown Buffalo on the Skyway

At Main Street, Church Street splits into a pair of one-way streets and becomes North and South Division Street. The route follows South Division eastward for two blocks to an intersection with Ellicott Street located one block north of [Sahlen Field](/wiki/Sahlen_Field "Sahlen Field"). At the junction, which includes the northern terminus of [NY 16](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_16 "New York State Route 16"), NY 5 turns northward, rejoining NY 5 westbound one block later at North Division. The route continues on Ellicott for nine blocks to the unidirectional East Tupper Street, where NY 5 westbound separates from the route once more. NY 5 eastbound, however, continues north on Ellicott for an additional block to the one-way Goodell Street. NY 5 heads west on Goodell for two blocks before turning north onto Main Street, rejoining NY 5 westbound at the intersection.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/20090412_Entranceways_at_Main_Street_at_Lamarck_Drive_and_Smallwood_Drive_from_east.JPG/250px-20090412_Entranceways_at_Main_Street_at_Lamarck_Drive_and_Smallwood_Drive_from_east.JPG)](/wiki/File:20090412_Entranceways_at_Main_Street_at_Lamarck_Drive_and_Smallwood_Drive_from_east.JPG)

NY 5 heading past the [Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places")-listed [Entranceways at Main Street at Lamarck Drive and Smallwood Drive](/wiki/Entranceways_at_Main_Street_at_Lamarck_Drive_and_Smallwood_Drive "Entranceways at Main Street at Lamarck Drive and Smallwood Drive") in [Snyder](/wiki/Snyder,_New_York "Snyder, New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/NY5EB_JCT_NY277.jpg/250px-NY5EB_JCT_NY277.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY5EB_JCT_NY277.jpg)

EB NY 5 at its junction with [NY 277](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_277 "New York State Route 277") near [Williamsville](/wiki/Williamsville,_New_York "Williamsville, New York").

The route continues along Main Street throughout the remainder of its length in Buffalo, cutting through the city diagonally from southwest to northeast until it enters the town of [Amherst](/wiki/Amherst,_New_York "Amherst, New York") at the intersection of Bailey Avenue ([US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_New_York "U.S. Route 62 in New York")) at the south campus of the [University at Buffalo](/wiki/University_at_Buffalo "University at Buffalo").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

### Buffalo to Avon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Buffalo to Avon")]

Once leaving the city of Buffalo, NY 5 heads east through the densely populated suburban town of [Amherst](/wiki/Amherst,_New_York "Amherst, New York"), including the hamlets of [Snyder](/wiki/Snyder,_New_York "Snyder, New York") and [Eggertsville](/wiki/Eggertsville,_New_York "Eggertsville, New York") and the village of [Williamsville](/wiki/Williamsville,_New_York "Williamsville, New York") and is heavily developed through the entire length of the town, particularly at the intersection with Transit Road ([NY 78](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_78 "New York State Route 78")). In the town of [Clarence](/wiki/Clarence,_New_York "Clarence, New York"), the road dips into a significant depression known as [Clarence Hollow](/wiki/Clarence_(CDP),_New_York "Clarence (CDP), New York"). Once leaving Clarence, NY 5 goes through predominantly rural areas until reaching the city of [Batavia](/wiki/Batavia,_New_York "Batavia, New York") in [Genesee County](/wiki/Genesee_County,_New_York "Genesee County, New York"), closely paralleling the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") through much of the county. The road travels eastward until reaching [Livingston County](/wiki/Livingston_County,_New_York "Livingston County, New York") and the village of [Caledonia](/wiki/Caledonia_(village),_New_York "Caledonia (village), New York").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

NY 5 heads southeast from the village of Caledonia, paralleling the former [right-of-way](/wiki/Right-of-way_(railroad) "Right-of-way (railroad)") of an [Erie–Lackawanna Railroad](/wiki/Erie%E2%80%93Lackawanna_Railroad "Erie–Lackawanna Railroad") branch line that connected the villages of Caledonia and [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York") as it heads through spacious fields containing little more than farmland. At a rural intersection controlled by single-head flashing [traffic signals](/wiki/Traffic_signals "Traffic signals") west of Avon, NY 5 meets [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") for the second time. The routes embark on a second [concurrency](/wiki/Concurrency_(road) "Concurrency (road)"), merging onto the right-of-way of NY 5 as they cross the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") and enter both the [town](/wiki/Avon_(town),_New_York "Avon (town), New York") and [village](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York") of Avon.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

### Avon to Ontario County

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Avon to Ontario County")]

US 20 and NY 5 become West Main Street upon entering the village, underscoring the road's status as the primary east–west highway through the town. The route continues southeast from the Genesee, passing through the forested but sparsely populated western area of the village. As the route approaches the [Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad](/wiki/Livonia,_Avon_and_Lakeville_Railroad "Livonia, Avon and Lakeville Railroad") (LAL) [grade crossing](/wiki/Grade_crossing "Grade crossing"), the number of homes increases rapidly, only to be replaced by businesses in the area surrounding the LAL mainline. Located on the western edge of this transition is [NY 39](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_39 "New York State Route 39"), which terminates at this junction. Past the tracks, West Main intersects Rochester Street, a locally important north–south two-lane arterial that continues north of the village to the [Rochester](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York "Rochester, New York") suburb of [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton,_Monroe_County,_New_York "Brighton, Monroe County, New York") as East River Road. Shortly after this intersection, the homes return, following US 20 and NY 5 as West Main enters the heart of the village.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_444.jpg/250px-US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_444.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_444.jpg)

US 20 and NY 5 eastbound at NY 444 near Bloomfield

In the centre of Avon, West Main feeds into Park Place, a large [traffic circle](/wiki/Traffic_circle "Traffic circle") providing access to two local streets from US 20 and NY 5. The routes follow the circle [counterclockwise](/wiki/Counterclockwise "Counterclockwise"), departing the roundabout on East Main Street. The street proceeds east, passing through four blocks of densely populated neighbourhoods before exiting the village and abruptly entering vast, barren fields to the east. US 20 and NY 5, now named Avon–Lima Road, intersects [NY 15](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15 "New York State Route 15") two miles (3 km) to the east in East Avon, a community based around the intersection and the streets comprising it, and connects to [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") at exit 10 a half-mile from NY 15. Continuing, the road intersects several county routes over the next four miles (6  km) before becoming West Main Street once more, this time for the village of [Lima](/wiki/Lima_(village),_New_York "Lima (village), New York"). At an intersection with [NY 15A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_15A "New York State Route 15A") in the village centre, US 20 and NY 5 become East Main Street, retaining the name to the [Ontario County](/wiki/Ontario_County,_New_York "Ontario County, New York") line at [Honeoye Creek](/wiki/Honeoye_Creek "Honeoye Creek").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

### Western Ontario County

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Western Ontario County")]

In the town of [West Bloomfield](/wiki/West_Bloomfield,_New_York "West Bloomfield, New York"), US 20 and NY 5 go unnamed as they proceed eastward. Roughly one mile from the county line in the hamlet of West Bloomfield, US 20 and NY 5 meet the southern terminus of [NY 65](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_65 "New York State Route 65"). Exiting the hamlet, US 20 and NY 5 head through another area dominated by open land, intersecting Elton Road before passing seamlessly into [East Bloomfield](/wiki/East_Bloomfield,_New_York "East Bloomfield, New York"). A mile and a half from the town line, US 20 and NY 5 intersect [NY 64](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_64 "New York State Route 64"), a road running northwest–southeast from the [Monroe County](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York") line south to US 20 and NY 5. NY 64, whose right-of-way ends at US 20 and NY 5 at the foot of a small hill, joins the two routes eastward on a triple overlap, entering the [village of Bloomfield](/wiki/Bloomfield,_New_York "Bloomfield, New York") and intersecting the southern terminus of [NY 444](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_444 "New York State Route 444") south of the portion of Bloomfield once known as Holcomb. Near the junction with NY 444, US 20, NY 5 and NY 64 take-ups on a due east alignment, absorbing the routing used by Gauss Road west of this point.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_21_w_split.jpg/250px-US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_21_w_split.jpg)](/wiki/File:US_20_and_NY_5_at_NY_21_w_split.jpg)

West end of the NY 21 overlaps as seen from US 20 and NY 5 westbound

A mile to the east at Whalen Road, NY 64 separates from US 20 and NY 5, following the road, and [US 20A](/wiki/U.S._Route_20A_(New_York) "U.S. Route 20A (New York)"), which has its eastern terminus at this intersection, south toward [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol,_New_York "Bristol, New York"). US 20 and NY 5 continue through rural Ontario County before splitting from its easterly alignment at an intersection four miles (6  km) east of US 20A and NY 64 in the [town of Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(town),_New_York "Canandaigua (town), New York"). West Avenue, the former routing of US 20 and NY 5 into [downtown Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York"), continues east from the junction while US 20 and NY 5 turn south onto a bypass around Canandaigua.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

#### Canandaigua area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Canandaigua area")]

Half a mile from the start of the bypass and a short distance before the arterial makes a turn eastward to traverse [Canandaigua Lake](/wiki/Canandaigua_Lake "Canandaigua Lake") to the southeast, US 20 and NY 5 meets [NY 21](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_21 "New York State Route 21") at a four-way intersection. Like US 20 and NY 5, NY 21 once continued directly into downtown, in this instance via Bristol Street to the east of the junction, but now follows US 20 and NY 5 along the east–west leg of the bypass. Past Bristol Street, the bypass widens from two to four lanes and, after meeting two local streets, enters the city of Canandaigua as the [limited-access](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") Western Boulevard, albeit with no exits. The route loses this distinction before intersecting South Main Street, where US 20, NY 5 and NY 21 meet the southern terminus of [NY 332](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_332 "New York State Route 332"). NY 21 departs the bypass, following NY 332 into downtown, while US 20 and NY 5 continue onto the four-lane, median separated Eastern Boulevard, the original section of the US 20 and NY 5 bypasses of Canandaigua. The roadway acts a centre of commerce for the city, sporting [restaurants](/wiki/Restaurant "Restaurant"), [hotels](/wiki/Hotel "Hotel"), and [supermarkets](/wiki/Supermarket "Supermarket") along its length within the city limits.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

Upon exiting the city, the establishments become a pair of [shopping plazas](/wiki/Shopping_plaza "Shopping plaza") centred around the intersection with the northern terminus of [NY 364](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_364 "New York State Route 364"). Across the town line in [Hopewell](/wiki/Hopewell,_New_York "Hopewell, New York") a quarter-mile to the east, a third plaza, anchored by [Runnings](/wiki/Runnings "Runnings"), formerly a [Walmart](/wiki/Walmart "Walmart"), dominates the northeast corner of [CR 10](/wiki/County_Route_10_(Ontario_County,_New_York) "County Route 10 (Ontario County, New York)") and Eastern Boulevard. On the adjacent parcel is another plaza containing the current Walmart. At the entrance to the second plaza, US 20 and NY 5 intersect Lakeshore Drive, the former routing of US 20 and NY 5 to the south of the bypass. Past the junction, the divided highway comes to an end and, after another half-mile, narrows to two lanes.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

### Canandaigua to Auburn

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Canandaigua to Auburn")]

Deeper into Hopewell, the area surrounding US 20 and NY 5 become rural once more. Roughly 1.5 miles (2.4 km) from the end of the bypass, US 20 and NY 5 meet [NY 247](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_247 "New York State Route 247"). After 10.3 miles (16.6 km) of eastward progression through open land, the routes meet the northern terminus of both [NY 14A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14A "New York State Route 14A") and [NY 245](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_245 "New York State Route 245"), concurrent at this location, in the [town of Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(town),_New_York "Geneva (town), New York"). A half-mile past NY 14A and NY 245, US 20 and NY 5 enter the [city of Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(city),_New_York "Geneva (city), New York") and widens into a four-lane road. In the city, US 20 and NY 5 intersect [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") by way of a pseudo-interchange on the bank of [Seneca Lake](/wiki/Seneca_Lake_(New_York) "Seneca Lake (New York)"). US 20 and NY 5 turns into a divided highway again at this point. At the interchange, [NY 14 Truck](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14_Truck "New York State Route 14 Truck") joins US 20 and NY 5, bypassing a sharp turn on NY 14 downtown. US 20 and NY 5 continues as a divided highway around the northern tip of Seneca Lake, crossing the [Preemption Line](/wiki/Preemption_Line "Preemption Line") and entering [Seneca County](/wiki/Seneca_County,_New_York "Seneca County, New York") at its midpoint. A mile to the east in [East Geneva](/wiki/East_Geneva,_New_York "East Geneva, New York"), just east of a railroad underpass, US 20 and NY 5 meet the northern terminus of [NY 96A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96A "New York State Route 96A") at a former [trumpet interchange](/wiki/Trumpet_interchange "Trumpet interchange") that has been converted to an intersection with a traffic signal.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/West_of_Auburn%2C_NY.jpg/250px-West_of_Auburn%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:West_of_Auburn,_NY.jpg)

US 20, NY 5 and NY 414 in [Seneca Falls](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(hamlet),_New_York "Seneca Falls (hamlet), New York")

From NY 96A east to the [village of Waterloo](/wiki/Waterloo_(village),_New_York "Waterloo (village), New York"), a distance of roughly four miles, US 20 and NY 5 become a two-lane road and run parallel to the [Cayuga–Seneca Canal](/wiki/Cayuga%E2%80%93Seneca_Canal "Cayuga–Seneca Canal"). In Waterloo, the concurrency meets [NY 96](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_96 "New York State Route 96") in the village centre. East of the village, the distance between the canal and the roadway decreases, making US 20 and NY 5 the closest road to the water for the next one and a half miles to [NY 414](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_414 "New York State Route 414") in the [town of Seneca Falls](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(town),_New_York "Seneca Falls (town), New York"). NY 414 joins US 20 and NY 5, overlapping the road for 4.3 miles (6.9 km) into the [hamlet of Seneca Falls](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(hamlet),_New_York "Seneca Falls (hamlet), New York"). At Cayuga Street, NY 414 turns south, crossing the water body that is the canal and the [Seneca River](/wiki/Seneca_River_(New_York) "Seneca River (New York)") and becoming Ovid Street while US 20 and NY 5 turn north onto Cayuga, following the street around the small [Van Cleef Lake](/w/index.php?title=Van_Cleef_Lake&action=edit&redlink=1 "Van Cleef Lake (page does not exist)"), through the [Finger Lakes Railway](/wiki/Finger_Lakes_Railway "Finger Lakes Railway") grade crossing, and exiting the hamlet. Three miles from NY 414, just west of [Montezuma National Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Montezuma_National_Wildlife_Refuge "Montezuma National Wildlife Refuge"), US 20 and NY 5 meet [NY 318](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_318 "New York State Route 318") and [NY 89](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_89 "New York State Route 89") at intersections just 0.1 miles (0.16 km) apart. The intersection with NY 89 has a traffic signal.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

About three miles (5 km) later, after entering Cayuga County over the [Seneca River](/wiki/Seneca_River_(New_York) "Seneca River (New York)")/[Cayuga and Seneca Canal](/wiki/Cayuga_and_Seneca_Canal "Cayuga and Seneca Canal"), just north of [Cayuga Lake](/wiki/Cayuga_Lake "Cayuga Lake"), the two routes meet [NY 90](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_90 "New York State Route 90") in the [town of Aurelius](/wiki/Aurelius,_New_York "Aurelius, New York"), at a traffic signal. A few miles east of that intersection, the highway meets the Finger Lakes Railway again but crosses it via an [overpass](/wiki/Overpass "Overpass"). The routes continue eastward through [Aurelius](/wiki/Aurelius,_New_York "Aurelius, New York") to the [city of Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York") where it turns into a divided four-lane highway again. On the western edge of the city, just after passing Finger Lakes Mall, US 20 and NY 5 meet the eastern terminus of [NY 326](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_326 "New York State Route 326"), which is also a four-lane divided highway for a short stretch. In downtown Auburn, US 20 and NY 5's east and west lanes split apart from each other for a short distance as an arterial over the alignments of Clark Street, Franklin Street, and Grant Avenue. The arterial runs concurrent with [NY 38](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_38 "New York State Route 38") for 0.2 miles (0.3 km). NY 38 then splits from the concurrency and joins [NY 34](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_34 "New York State Route 34"). A quarter-mile to the east, US 20 separates from NY 5 at the northern terminus of [NY 38A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_38A "New York State Route 38A"). NY 5 continues after the split as the four-lane Grant Avenue passing by a high number of shopping areas.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

### Auburn to Syracuse

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Auburn to Syracuse")]

From [NY 174](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_174 "New York State Route 174") in [Camillus](/wiki/Camillus,_New_York_(village) "Camillus, New York (village)") to [Fairmount](/wiki/Fairmount,_New_York "Fairmount, New York"), NY 5 is a 5-mile (8 km) [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") traversing the western suburbs of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"). At one time, the highway was to be extended to West Street in Syracuse, via the current Grand Avenue.[[11]](#cite_note-syr_hist-11) The freeway has partial access to [NY 173](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_173 "New York State Route 173") from westbound NY 5. East of NY 173, the freeway connects to [NY 695](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_695 "New York State Route 695") at a [directional T interchange](/wiki/Directional_T_interchange "Directional T interchange") and passes over [NY 297](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_297 "New York State Route 297") without access. East of Fairmount, NY 5 alters to the south before turning east onto West Genesee Street and converting to [grade-level intersections](/wiki/At-grade_intersection "At-grade intersection").[[12]](#cite_note-NY_5_in_Syracuse-12)

In Syracuse, NY 5 is parallel to [I-690](/wiki/Interstate_690 "Interstate 690") for much of its routing but never encounters the highway, thus making the north–south streets that intersect NY 5 entry points to and from I-690. In downtown Syracuse, West Genesee Street becomes James Street. At the southern tip of the interchange between I-690 and [I-81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York"), NY 5 transfers onto Erie Boulevard and intersects State Street ([US 11](/wiki/U.S._Route_11_in_New_York "U.S. Route 11 in New York")), but passes under I-81 without access.[[12]](#cite_note-NY_5_in_Syracuse-12) From the downtown area to [DeWitt](/wiki/DeWitt,_New_York "DeWitt, New York"), NY 5 is divided.[[13]](#cite_note-13) At the Syracuse–DeWitt boundary, NY 5 intersects [NY 635](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_635 "New York State Route 635") and eastward, it curves to a southeast course. Near the former [Shoppingtown Mall](/wiki/Shoppingtown_Mall "Shoppingtown Mall"), NY 5 turns east onto Genesee Street to begin an overlap with [NY 92](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_92 "New York State Route 92"). Less than a mile east of the mall, NY 5 and NY 92 intersect [I-481](/wiki/Interstate_481 "Interstate 481") at a [cloverleaf interchange](/wiki/Cloverleaf_interchange "Cloverleaf interchange").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Utica_Arterial_-_May_2015.jpg/250px-Utica_Arterial_-_May_2015.jpg)](/wiki/File:Utica_Arterial_-_May_2015.jpg)

The Utica Arterial under construction in May 2015

### Syracuse to Utica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Syracuse to Utica")]

NY 5 and NY 92 remain concurrent up to Highbridge Road, where NY 92 splits from NY 5 and heads southeast to [Manlius](/wiki/Manlius_(village),_New_York "Manlius (village), New York"). The segment of the overlap with NY 92 between I-481 and the eastern split is the busiest area of NY 5 in the Syracuse area and in all of Onondaga County.[[15]](#cite_note-traffic-15) Past the split, NY 5 continues east through Onondaga and [Madison](/wiki/Madison_County,_New_York "Madison County, New York") counties, passing [Fayetteville](/wiki/Fayetteville,_New_York "Fayetteville, New York"), [Chittenango](/wiki/Chittenango,_New_York "Chittenango, New York"), and [Canastota](/wiki/Canastota,_New_York "Canastota, New York") before entering the vicinity of [Oneida](/wiki/Oneida,_New_York "Oneida, New York"). West of the city, NY 5 intersects [NY 365A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_365A "New York State Route 365A"), a spur route of [NY 365](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_365 "New York State Route 365") leading directly into downtown. To the east, NY 5 (which forms the southern boundary of the city) meets [NY 46](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_46 "New York State Route 46") before crossing over [Oneida Creek](/wiki/Oneida_Creek "Oneida Creek") and into [Oneida County](/wiki/Oneida_County,_New_York "Oneida County, New York").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

Just past the county line in [Oneida Castle](/wiki/Oneida_Castle,_New_York "Oneida Castle, New York"), NY 5 intersects NY 365, a route leading northward to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") in [Verona](/wiki/Verona,_New_York "Verona, New York"). NY 5 presses on, passing through the city of [Sherrill](/wiki/Sherrill,_New_York "Sherrill, New York") and the village of [Vernon](/wiki/Vernon_(village),_New_York "Vernon (village), New York") (briefly overlapping [NY 31](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_31 "New York State Route 31")) and the town of [Westmoreland](/wiki/Westmoreland,_New_York "Westmoreland, New York") to the town of [Kirkland](/wiki/Kirkland,_New_York "Kirkland, New York"), where NY 5 intersects [NY 233](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_233 "New York State Route 233"), crosses over [Oriskany Creek](/wiki/Oriskany_Creek "Oriskany Creek"), and meets the western terminus of NY 5B. The spur of NY 5 later rejoins its parent yards from where [NY 5A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5A "New York State Route 5A") departs NY 5 to serve western [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York"). NY 5 itself continues eastward through [New Hartford](/wiki/New_Hartford_(town),_New_York "New Hartford (town), New York"), meeting [NY 12B](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_12B "New York State Route 12B") before merging with [NY 12](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_12 "New York State Route 12") at Genesee Street. Both routes continue eastward across the [Sauquoit Creek](/wiki/Sauquoit_Creek "Sauquoit Creek") into [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

### Utica to Albany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Utica to Albany")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/State_Street%2C_Schenctady%2C_NY.jpg/250px-State_Street%2C_Schenctady%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:State_Street,_Schenctady,_NY.jpg)

State Street in downtown Schenectady

NY 5 enters the city of Utica on a concurrency with NY 12 heading in a northeast direction. It shortly picks up [NY 8](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_8 "New York State Route 8"), and all three cross the city together. NY 5 also intersects with the terminus of [NY 840](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_840 "New York State Route 840") at this point. Just south of the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway"), [I-790](/wiki/Interstate_790 "Interstate 790") begins as a short expressway, also including NY 5, NY 8, and NY 12. After crossing out of the city, they meet the Thruway, with NY 8 and 12 continuing northeast, while I-790 and NY 5 turns to the east-south-east, picking up the tail-end of [NY 49](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_49 "New York State Route 49"). These three, still as an expressway, straddle each side of the Thruway for a short way, with I-790 technically ending at the ramps for I-90. NY 5 continues to the end of the expressway, only a few hundred feet later, dropping to Leland Avenue. A few hundred feet to the north of the Thruway, NY 5 turns eastward again to continue down Herkimer Road. It closely parallels the Thruway to [Herkimer](/wiki/Herkimer_(village),_New_York "Herkimer (village), New York"), where NY 5 moves slightly northward through the centre of the village, becoming State Street, while I-90 crosses the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") and goes south for a short distance. There is a short concurrency with [NY 28](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_28 "New York State Route 28") in the village.[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

After exiting Herkimer, NY 5 continues east, closely paralleling this time the canal, through the city of [Little Falls](/wiki/Little_Falls_(city),_New_York "Little Falls (city), New York") as Main Street, where two more concurrencies occur, with [NY 167](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_167 "New York State Route 167") and [NY 169](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_169 "New York State Route 169"). NY 5 continues to parallel the canal, and in some instances again, the Thruway, through [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam_(city),_New_York "Amsterdam (city), New York"), becoming Amsterdam Road all the way to [Scotia](/wiki/Scotia,_New_York "Scotia, New York"), where it crosses the canal into [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York") as Mohawk Avenue, turning into State Street upon entering the city limits. It continues fairly straight on a southeast course into [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") as Central Avenue until it reaches [Townsend Park](/wiki/Townsend_Park "Townsend Park"). At this point, NY 5 turns into Washington Avenue and all signage referring to NY 5 ceases. The [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") recognizes the route, however, as it continues down Washington Ave past the [New York State Capitol](/wiki/New_York_State_Capitol "New York State Capitol") building, turning south for a short distance as Eagle Street. NY 5 then continues east on State Street to Broadway, where it again turns south-east shortly before returning east on a small spur of Broadway, travelling underneath [US 9](/wiki/U.S._Route_9_in_New_York "U.S. Route 9 in New York") and [I-787](/wiki/Interstate_787 "Interstate 787"). NY 5 ends at the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River").[[10]](#cite_note-2008map-10)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

### Early roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Early roads")]

Soon after the end of the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution") in 1783, a surge of westward migration into [Central](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York") and [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") began. At the time, most travel west of the Albany area was by water. While rudimentary roads were laid out following the [Mohawk River](/wiki/Mohawk_River "Mohawk River"), there were no major land routes west of [Fort Schuyler](/wiki/Old_Fort_Schuyler "Old Fort Schuyler") (present-day [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York")), except for an old east–west [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") trail that was a simple footpath. By the late 1780s, many companies began to set up their operations in the new settlements in the Central and Western New York. As a result, there was a clamour for the building of the main road running west from Utica.[[16]](#cite_note-pioneer-16)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/NY_5_east_at_NY_46.jpg/250px-NY_5_east_at_NY_46.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_5_east_at_NY_46.jpg)

Junction of NY 5 and NY 46 in Oneida

On March 22, 1794, the [New York State Legislature](/wiki/New_York_State_Legislature "New York State Legislature") passed a law calling for the laying out and improvement of a public road from old Fort Schuyler on the Mohawk River to the settlement of [Canawaugus](/wiki/Canawaugus,_New_York "Canawaugus, New York") on the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River"), in as straight a line as the topography of the land would allow. This road was officially known as the "Great Genesee Road" and is one of the earliest state roads in New York,[[16]](#cite_note-pioneer-16) intended to provide access to the [New Military Tract](/wiki/New_Military_Tract "New Military Tract"). As planned, it generally followed the old Iroquois trail through [Oneida](/wiki/Oneida,_New_York "Oneida, New York"), [Manlius](/wiki/Manlius_(village),_New_York "Manlius (village), New York"), [Onondaga Valley](/wiki/Onondaga_Valley "Onondaga Valley") (south of modern [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York")), [Skaneateles](/wiki/Skaneateles_(village),_New_York "Skaneateles (village), New York"), [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York"), [Seneca Falls](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_(hamlet),_New_York "Seneca Falls (hamlet), New York"), [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(city),_New_York "Geneva (city), New York"), and [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York")[[17]](#cite_note-genesee-17) before ending at the Genesee River. Four years later, another legislative act authorized the extension of the Genesee Road to [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York").[[16]](#cite_note-pioneer-16)

By the end of the 18th century, while the Genesee Road had been greatly improved and saw heavy traffic, many portions were still substandard and some sections had still not been completed.[[17]](#cite_note-genesee-17)[[18]](#cite_note-hulbert-18) Partly because of this, and also because of the success of the [Lancaster Turnpike](/wiki/Lancaster_Turnpike "Lancaster Turnpike") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), the state outsourced the task of improving and maintaining the Genesee Road to a private company. On April 1, 1800, the [Seneca Road Company](/wiki/Seneca_Road_Company "Seneca Road Company") was chartered for this purpose and the portion of the Genesee Road from Utica to Canandaigua was improved and operated as a toll road known as the Seneca Turnpike,[[18]](#cite_note-hulbert-18) which was 157 miles (253 km) long and, at the time, the longest [turnpike](/wiki/Toll_road "Toll road") in the state.[[17]](#cite_note-genesee-17)[[19]](#cite_note-seneca-19) Three days later, the old road following the Mohawk River between Utica and [Schenectady](/wiki/Schenectady,_New_York "Schenectady, New York") also became a turnpike, known as the Mohawk Turnpike.[[18]](#cite_note-hulbert-18)

With the road leading from [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") northwest to Schenectady having been already established as a turnpike (the Albany and Schenectady Turnpike) in 1797, an all-turnpike route over good quality roads was now available from Albany to Canandaigua. The western extension of the Genesee Road to Buffalo soon followed suit and also became an improved [Macadam](/wiki/Macadam "Macadam") toll road,[[20]](#cite_note-20) the Ontario and Genesee Turnpike, in 1805. The Seneca Road Company was authorized to create a more northerly alternate route of the Seneca Turnpike in 1806.[[21]](#cite_note-tpke-21) This branch left the original turnpike east of Seneca Falls and crossed more level terrain through [Elbridge](/wiki/Elbridge_(town),_New_York "Elbridge (town), New York"), [Geddes](/wiki/Geddes,_New_York "Geddes, New York"), and [Fayetteville](/wiki/Fayetteville,_New_York "Fayetteville, New York") before rejoining the old path at [Chittenango](/wiki/Chittenango,_New_York "Chittenango, New York").[[17]](#cite_note-genesee-17)[[19]](#cite_note-seneca-19) As the city of [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York") developed, traffic patterns changed and the northern branch route became more heavily used than the original road.[[21]](#cite_note-tpke-21)

The construction and opening of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") in 1825 along the same alignment as the Albany to Buffalo route began to eat away at the revenues of these turnpike companies. In time, the turnpike business had become unprofitable and the companies were dissolved by 1852, causing the roads to revert to public control.[[21]](#cite_note-tpke-21) The Seneca Road Company dissolved in 1852. The old, southern path of the Seneca Turnpike is now Franklin Street and Old Seneca Turnpike from Auburn to [Marcellus](/wiki/Marcellus_(village),_New_York "Marcellus (village), New York"), [NY 175](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_175 "New York State Route 175") between Marcellus and [Onondaga Hill](/wiki/Onondaga_Hill,_New_York "Onondaga Hill, New York"), and [NY 173](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_173 "New York State Route 173") from there east to Chittenango.[[17]](#cite_note-genesee-17)[[19]](#cite_note-seneca-19)

### Designation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Designation")]

The improvement of the road from Buffalo southwest to Pennsylvania in the mid-19th century soon allowed for continuous travel across the entire state of New York. With the advent of the [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile"), the state began to take over and pave major thoroughfares at the beginning of the 20th century. In 1908, the state legislature created a statewide system of unsigned [legislative routes](/wiki/Legislative_route "Legislative route"). One of the routes assigned at this time was Route 6, an Albany–Buffalo highway that followed the path of the Genesee Road and the Seneca Turnpike from Buffalo to Utica, the Mohawk Turnpike between Utica and Schenectady, and the Albany and Schenectady Turnpike from Schenectady to Albany. From Auburn to Chittenango, Route 6 utilized most of the newer, northern branch of the Seneca Turnpike.[[22]](#cite_note-1909book-22)[[23]](#cite_note-1920book-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/NY_5_reassurance_shield_in_Williamsville_edited.jpg/250px-NY_5_reassurance_shield_in_Williamsville_edited.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_5_reassurance_shield_in_Williamsville_edited.jpg)

[Reassurance marker](/wiki/Reassurance_marker "Reassurance marker") on NY 5 in Williamsville

The automobile allowed people to quickly travel long distances and a way to mark routes became needed. One early means of marking routes was the establishment of various [auto trail](/wiki/Auto_trail "Auto trail") associations in the 1910s. These associations selected good quality roads and marked them with symbols or colors on telephone poles. Most of legislative Route 6 eventually became part of the [Yellowstone Trail](/wiki/Yellowstone_Trail "Yellowstone Trail"),[[24]](#cite_note-1926map-24) a cross-country auto trail established in 1912 that ran from [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)") to [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts").[[25]](#cite_note-25) In New York, the trail used modern [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York") from Pennsylvania to [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York"), most of modern NY 5 from Silver Creek to Albany, and modern US 20 again from Albany to Massachusetts.[[24]](#cite_note-1926map-24)

In 1924, following what other states did, New York began to assign route numbers to its main thoroughfares. The Albany to Buffalo portion of the Yellowstone Trail, which ran through the cities of Syracuse and Utica, was assigned the number NY 5A. The portion of the Yellowstone Trail southwest of Buffalo and east of Albany became part of NY 5, which bypassed Syracuse and Utica to the south. The Buffalo to Albany portion of NY 5's original alignment used a new road, Broadway Road, from Buffalo to [Avon](/wiki/Avon_(village),_New_York "Avon (village), New York") and the old [Cherry Valley Turnpike](/wiki/Cherry_Valley_Turnpike "Cherry Valley Turnpike") alignment from Skaneateles to Albany. In between Avon and Skaneateles, NY 5 and NY 5A overlapped.[[2]](#cite_note-1924nyt-2)[[24]](#cite_note-1926map-24)

By 1926, however, the Buffalo to Albany section of NY 5 was relocated onto the Genesee Road alignment, replacing NY 5A. NY 5's former, more southerly alignment was redesignated as [NY 7](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_7_(mid-1920s%E2%80%931927) "New York State Route 7 (mid-1920s–1927)").[[24]](#cite_note-1926map-24) In 1927, the establishment of the [U.S. Highway System](/wiki/U.S._Highway_System "U.S. Highway System") created more numbering changes. US 20, which mainly followed the Yellowstone Trail elsewhere in the country, was designated in New York along NY 5 southwest of Hamburg and east of Albany and along old NY 7 from Skaneateles to Albany. Between the towns of Hamburg and Avon, the new US 20 used an even more southerly alignment, running via East Aurora and Warsaw. This truncated both ends of NY 5 to [Athol Springs](/wiki/Athol_Springs,_New_York "Athol Springs, New York") (south of Buffalo in the town of Hamburg) in the west, and to Albany in the east.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In the [1930 state highway renumbering](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)"), NY 5 was truncated even further to begin in downtown Buffalo. The portion between Buffalo and Athol Springs was assigned as part of [NY 62](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_62_(1930%E2%80%931932) "New York State Route 62 (1930–1932)").[[27]](#cite_note-gb-27) Southwest of Buffalo, Southwestern Boulevard, an alternate route of US 20 between [Irving](/wiki/Irving,_New_York "Irving, New York") and [Big Tree](/wiki/Big_Tree,_New_York "Big Tree, New York") (east of Athol Springs) became [NY 20B](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_20B "New York State Route 20B").[[28]](#cite_note-1930map-28) Further southwest, another alternate route of US 20 between the Pennsylvania line and [Silver Creek](/wiki/Silver_Creek,_New_York "Silver Creek, New York"), running along the shore of Lake Erie, was designated as NY 20A.[[27]](#cite_note-gb-27) The NY 20A and NY 20B designations proved to be short-lived. [US 62](/wiki/U.S._Route_62_in_New_York "U.S. Route 62 in New York") was extended into New York c. 1932, causing NY 62 to be renumbered. Around the same time, US 20 was realigned to follow NY 20B from Irving to Big Tree. NY 5 was extended along part of old NY 62 to Athol Springs, from where it continued to the Pennsylvania state line by way of US 20's old routing to Irving and all of NY 20A.[[29]](#cite_note-1931map-29)[[30]](#cite_note-1932map-30)

### Expressway relocations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Expressway relocations")]

Originally, NY 5 entered Buffalo from the south on Fuhrmann Boulevard and Michigan Avenue and followed South Park Avenue and Main Street through the city before rejoining its modern alignment at Goodell Street. In the mid-1950s, a new [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway") was constructed along Fuhrmann Boulevard from [Lackawanna](/wiki/Lackawanna,_New_York "Lackawanna, New York") to the [Buffalo River](/wiki/Buffalo_River_(New_York) "Buffalo River (New York)"). At the river, the new roadway broke from Fuhrmann and continued directly into downtown, returning to grade level two blocks south of Niagara Square. The expressway, known as the Buffalo Skyway, became part of a rerouted NY 5 by 1956.[[31]](#cite_note-1954map-31)[[32]](#cite_note-1956map-32) Visually, the Skyway cuts off the city from the Buffalo inner harbor. In 2008 there was momentum to tear it down, but the momentum passed.[[33]](#cite_note-33) In 2019 a plan to remove part of the Skyway and close the rest to motorized traffic was proposed as part of a competition.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) This plan has drawn strong opinions both for and against the removal.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/NY_174_at_old_NY_5_in_Camillus.jpg/250px-NY_174_at_old_NY_5_in_Camillus.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY_174_at_old_NY_5_in_Camillus.jpg)

Approaching Genesee Street on NY 174 northbound in Camillus. Until the Camillus Bypass was constructed, NY 5 followed Genesee Street and NY 174 ended at this junction.

Farther east in Utica, construction began in the early 1950s on a new arterial highway—known as the North–South Arterial—through the city center. The new roadway bypassed NY 5, which was initially routed on Genesee Street and Herkimer Road through Utica.[[31]](#cite_note-1954map-31)[[37]](#cite_note-37) The first portion of the highway to open was the segment north of River Road, which was completed by 1956.[[32]](#cite_note-1956map-32) It was extended southward to Oriskany Street ([NY 5A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5A "New York State Route 5A")) by 1961[[38]](#cite_note-38) and completed entirely by 1964, at which time it became part of a rerouted NY 5 and [NY 12](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_12 "New York State Route 12").[[39]](#cite_note-1964map-39) Two portions of Genesee Street, from NY 12 in New Hartford to the Utica city line and from [NY 5S](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5S "New York State Route 5S") to Herkimer Road in Utica, remain state maintained to this day as unsigned NY 921E and NY 921C, respectively.[[40]](#cite_note-2009log-40)

In the [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York") suburbs of [Camillus](/wiki/Camillus_(town),_New_York "Camillus (town), New York") and [Geddes](/wiki/Geddes,_New_York "Geddes, New York"), NY 5 was initially routed on West Genesee Street between the villages of [Camillus](/wiki/Camillus_(village),_New_York "Camillus (village), New York") and [Solvay](/wiki/Solvay,_New_York "Solvay, New York").[[41]](#cite_note-1968map-41) Construction on a bypass of this segment of NY 5 began in the early 1970s[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) and was completed between [NY 695](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_695 "New York State Route 695") and Genesee Street by 1977.[[44]](#cite_note-1977map-44) By the following year, the freeway was open to traffic up to Hinsdale Road; however, NY 5 remained on Genesee Street between Hinsdale and the Solvay village limits.[[45]](#cite_note-1978topo-45) The remainder of the Camillus Bypass was completed c. 1979, at which time NY 5 was realigned to follow the freeway.[[45]](#cite_note-1978topo-45)[[46]](#cite_note-1979map-46) Genesee Street is now largely maintained by Onondaga County as CR 98; however, two portions of the street remain state maintained. Near the western end of the expressway, the former routing of NY 5 became part of an extended [NY 174](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_174 "New York State Route 174").[[47]](#cite_note-camillus_quad-47)[[48]](#cite_note-syracuse_west_quad-48) Between the Camillus town line and the eastern end of the bypass, Genesee Street is unsigned NY 930W.[[40]](#cite_note-2009log-40) This particular expressway was meant to link [Downtown Syracuse](/wiki/Downtown_Syracuse "Downtown Syracuse") to [Auburn](/wiki/Auburn,_New_York "Auburn, New York"), but was never completed past Camillus or Fairmount. Several incomplete ramps mark both ends of this expressway section.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Old_5_and_20.JPG/250px-Old_5_and_20.JPG)](/wiki/File:Old_5_and_20.JPG)

Lakeshore Drive in Canandaigua

Smaller realignments also took place in other cities along the route. In [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(city),_New_York "Canandaigua (city), New York"), NY 5 originally entered the city on West Avenue and followed South Main Street and Lakeshore Drive through the city limits before rejoining its current routing in [Hopewell](/wiki/Hopewell,_New_York "Hopewell, New York"). In the mid-1950s, a new bypass was built north of Lakeshore Drive from South Main Street to Hopewell. It became part of a realigned NY 5 by 1956.[[31]](#cite_note-1954map-31)[[32]](#cite_note-1956map-32) The remainder of the bypass around the southwestern extents of the city was built in the late 1970s and early 1980s.[[46]](#cite_note-1979map-46)[[50]](#cite_note-1981map-50) The former routing of NY 5 on South Main Street remains state maintained as [NY 942T](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_942T "New York State Route 942T");[[40]](#cite_note-2009log-40) until 1996, the portion of West Avenue between the west end of the bypass and the Canandaigua city line was maintained by the [New York State Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_Transportation "New York State Department of Transportation") (NYSDOT) as NY 942W. Even though maintenance of the road had been transferred to the town of [Canandaigua](/wiki/Canandaigua_(town),_New_York "Canandaigua (town), New York") in 1996,[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) the designation remained in NYSDOT documents until 2007.[[53]](#cite_note-2007tdr-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

In [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva_(city),_New_York "Geneva (city), New York"), NY 5 was initially routed on East North Street and Border City Road, overlapping [NY 14](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_14 "New York State Route 14") through the city and rejoining its modern routing in East Geneva.[[28]](#cite_note-1930map-28) The overlap was eliminated c. 1931 when NY 5 was moved onto a new roadway located along the edge of [Seneca Lake](/wiki/Seneca_Lake_(New_York) "Seneca Lake (New York)").[[28]](#cite_note-1930map-28)[[29]](#cite_note-1931map-29) NY 5 was realigned again in the 1960s to use a new [divided highway](/wiki/Divided_highway "Divided highway") built midway between the lake shore road and Border City Road.[[41]](#cite_note-1968map-41)[[55]](#cite_note-55) Border City Road is now maintained by Seneca County as [CR 110](/wiki/County_Route_110_(Seneca_County,_New_York) "County Route 110 (Seneca County, New York)").[[56]](#cite_note-geneva_north_quad-56)

In April 2014 work began on a $68.3 million project to replace the viaduct over Columbia Street, Lafayette Streets, and Oriskany Boulevard (NY 5A and NY 5S) in Utica. The nearly one mile stretch had signalized at-grade intersections that had been causing safety concerns and some fatalities. In addition to the replacement of the viaduct, the alignment of the arterial was straightened, a new [single point urban interchange](/wiki/Single_point_urban_interchange "Single point urban interchange") was built at Court Street, and a pedestrian bridge was built across the roadway. The pedestrian bridge was opened by December 2014, and the remainder of the project was completed by October 2017.[[57]](#cite_note-NY5_8_12_arterial-57)[[58]](#cite_note-NSAHR-58)

## Suffixed routes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Suffixed routes")]

NY 5 has three suffixed routes, all located in Oneida County, with NY 5S extending eastward into three other counties. The NY 5A designation was also used in the past for two other routes.

### NY 5A

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: NY 5A")]

* The NY 5A designation has been used for three distinct highways:
  + The first NY 5A was a short-lived designation for the portion of modern NY 5 east of [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"). When it existed from 1924 to the mid-1920s, NY 5 was routed on what is now [US 20](/wiki/U.S._Route_20_in_New_York "U.S. Route 20 in New York").[[2]](#cite_note-1924nyt-2)[[24]](#cite_note-1926map-24)
  + The second NY 5A was a northerly alternate route of NY 5 between [Aurelius](/wiki/Aurelius,_New_York "Aurelius, New York") and [Sennett](/wiki/Sennett,_New_York "Sennett, New York") in [Cayuga County](/wiki/Cayuga_County,_New_York "Cayuga County, New York"). It was assigned c. 1933[[30]](#cite_note-1932map-30)[[59]](#cite_note-59) and renumbered to NY 135 c. 1937.[[60]](#cite_note-1936map-60)[[61]](#cite_note-1937map-61) That route was removed c. 1939.[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) Its former routing is now maintained by Cayuga County as [CR 10A](/wiki/County_Route_10A_(Cayuga_County,_New_York) "County Route 10A (Cayuga County, New York)"), [CR 10B](/wiki/County_Route_10B_(Cayuga_County,_New_York) "County Route 10B (Cayuga County, New York)"), and [CR 10C](/wiki/County_Route_10C_(Cayuga_County,_New_York) "County Route 10C (Cayuga County, New York)").[[64]](#cite_note-auburn_quad-64)[[65]](#cite_note-cayuga_quad-65)
  + [![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/NY5A_West_End_-_NY5_NY5B_Signs_%2842805916581%29.jpg/250px-NY5A_West_End_-_NY5_NY5B_Signs_%2842805916581%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:NY5A_West_End_-_NY5_NY5B_Signs_(42805916581).jpg)

    Shields at western terminus of NY 5A

The [current NY 5A](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5A "New York State Route 5A") is a short 5.59-mile-long (9.00 km) alternate route of NY 5 between [New Hartford](/wiki/New_Hartford_(town),_New_York "New Hartford (town), New York") and downtown [Utica](/wiki/Utica,_New_York "Utica, New York") in [Oneida County](/wiki/Oneida_County,_New_York "Oneida County, New York").[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1) It was assigned in the mid-1930s.[[60]](#cite_note-1936map-60)[[66]](#cite_note-66) At its eastern end, NY 5A becomes NY 5S at an interchange with Interstate 790 (I-790), NY 5, NY 8, and NY 12. The route is four lanes wide and passes through mostly commercial areas and connects NY 5 to NY 840 and NY 69.

### NY 5B

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: NY 5B")]

NY 5B is a 3.12-mile-long (5.02 km) alternate route southwest of Utica in Oneida County, connecting NY 5 to [NY 12B](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_12B "New York State Route 12B").[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1) The route was assigned in April 1935.[[67]](#cite_note-1935change-67)
The entire route is in [Oneida County](/wiki/Oneida_County,_New_York "Oneida County, New York").

### NY 5S

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=New_York_State_Route_5&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: NY 5S")]

* [NY 5S](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_5S "New York State Route 5S") (72.92 miles or 117.35 kilometres) is an alternate route of NY 5 on the south side of the [Mohawk River](/wiki/Mohawk_River "Mohawk River") between Utica, Oneida County, and [Rotterdam](/wiki/Rotterdam_(town),_New_York "Rotterdam (town), New York"), [Schenectady County](/wiki/Schenectady_County,_New_York "Schenectady County, New York"). The route parallels NY 5 (which follows the north side of the river) and is partially a [limited-access highway](/wiki/Limited-access_highway "Limited-access highway").[[1]](#cite_note-2014tdr-1) It was assigned as part of the [1930 renumbering of state highways in New York](/wiki/1930_state_highway_renumbering_(New_York) "1930 state highway renumbering (New York)").[[68]](#cite_note-68)
